# Preface. What this series builds {.unnumbered}

This is my public homelab guide: one Raspberry Pi, one private
[Tailscale](https://tailscale.com) network, and a handful of useful services
added one at a time. The specifics come from the hardware and devices I
have access to, but the pattern is meant for anyone building a similar beginner
homelab. No port forwarding, no public dashboard hanging off the internet, no
mystery pile of commands you cannot reproduce later.

By the end, following along gives you an always-on Pi that serves spoken audio,
blocks ads for the house, provides one dashboard URL, works from mobile devices,
and can route traffic through a VPN when that makes sense. The chapters
are short on purpose: do one job, verify it, move on.

## Volume I: Building the homelab

1. **[Foundation: your Pi and your Tailscale network](chapter-1-foundation.qmd).**
   Flash a headless 64-bit Linux with key-based SSH, install Docker, and put the
   Pi, a laptop/desktop, and a phone on one private Tailscale network with MagicDNS,
   reachable by name from anywhere.
2. **[Streaming audiobooks with Audiobookshelf](chapter-2-audiobookshelf.qmd).**
   Serve spoken audio from the Pi, streaming to a phone with proper resume
   tracking. The running example is my own: a language course I ripped from CD so
   I can study from anywhere without carrying the discs.
3. **[Network-wide ad blocking with Pi-hole](chapter-3-pihole.qmd).** Run Pi-hole
   as a DNS server and point your router at it, so every device on your home
   network is ad-blocked with zero per-device setup.
4. **[Pretty URLs: a reverse proxy and local DNS](chapter-4-pretty-urls.qmd).**
   Add Caddy and local DNS so you can type `https://pihole.home` and
   `https://abs.home` on your tailnet, no ports or IPs, with real HTTPS.
5. **[A one-URL dashboard](chapter-5-dashboard.qmd).** Add Homepage and Portainer,
   slotted into Caddy at `https://home.home` as a single landing page.

## Volume II: VPNs and remote access

6. **[Why a VPN, and Mullvad vs. a standard app like Aura](chapter-6-vpn.qmd).**
   What a VPN does, how a mainstream app (I started on Aura) differs from
   Mullvad, and why Mullvad's portable WireGuard configs are what let a VPN mesh
   with a homelab, so you can pick the right kind.
7. **[Exit nodes, the Mullvad add-on, and what I run](chapter-7-away-from-home.qmd).**
   The one rule for why homelab access, Pi-hole, and a privacy VPN can't all run
   at once with two apps, the switch-per-activity setup I live with, what a
   Tailscale exit node is, and the paid add-on that buys you both at once.

## Volume III: Extras

8. **[Remoting into machines from a phone](chapter-8-remoting-phone.qmd).**
   Reach your Pi's terminal (Termius) and a full desktop (NoMachine) from your
   phone or tablet, by Tailscale machine name.
9. **[Wiring a phone into Linux machines](chapter-9-phone-linux.qmd).**
   LocalSend and KDE Connect for file transfer and clipboard sharing over
   Tailscale, plus the USB fallback for bulk photo offload.

Two **appendices** back the parts up:
[Appendix A](appendix-a-compose-explained.qmd) explains every Docker Compose file
line by line (kept out of the main chapters so they stay readable), and
[Appendix B](appendix-b-verify.qmd) is a "what should just work" reference, every
URL and the one-line check that proves it's healthy, ideal as a post-reboot smoke
test.

## The hardware and accounts

- **The always-on server.** I'm running this on a **Raspberry Pi 4 Model B
  (8 GB RAM)** with a **32 GB microSD card**, in an **Argon ONE M.2 Aluminum
  case** (passive-plus-fan cooling, with an M.2 slot for a future SSD), on
  **Ubuntu Server 26.04 LTS (64-bit), headless** (no desktop, just SSH). That's
  what I happen to use, and what these steps are written and tested on. You
  don't need the exact same kit; anything similar (a Pi 4 or 5 with a few GB of
  RAM and a 16 GB+ card, on a Debian/Ubuntu base) will follow along. There's
  plenty of headroom here, since all the containers, configs, and a ripped audio
  course together use only a few GB.
- **A laptop/desktop** (Linux assumed) to flash the card and use as a client.
- **A free Tailscale account.** One account, signed into on every device, is the
  connective tissue for the entire series.
- **A phone.** My examples use an **iPhone**, because that is the phone I have
  for testing. The same apps have Android builds unless a chapter calls out an
  iOS-specific limitation, so use the equivalent app for the reader's platform.
- **Docker** on the Pi, installed once in Chapter 1, reused by every later chapter.
- A Mullvad subscription *or* the Tailscale Mullvad add-on for Chapters 6 and 7
  (both optional, ~$5/month each; Chapter 6 picks the kind of VPN and Chapter 7
  picks how it meets your homelab).

## Conventions used throughout

- I'm running **Ubuntu Server 26.04 LTS** on the Pi, which is a
  **Debian/Ubuntu-flavored** Linux, so commands use `apt`, `systemd`, and
  `docker`.
- Commands prefixed with `sudo` need administrator rights; the rest run as your
  normal user.
- **The example phone is an iPhone.** I write from the device I can test:
  Audiobookshelf, Tailscale, Termius, NoMachine, and LocalSend on iOS. That is
  not an assumption about the reader's phone. Where the Android path differs, I
  call it out; otherwise use the same app on the platform at hand.
- **Placeholders, substitute your own.** Wherever you see a Tailscale address
  like `100.x.y.z`, run `tailscale ip -4` on that machine to get the real one.
  This guide names the Raspberry Pi `homelab` on the tailnet; its full DNS name
  is therefore `homelab.<your-tailnet>.ts.net`, where `<your-tailnet>` is the
  suffix shown on the **DNS** page of the Tailscale admin console (something
  like `tail1a2b3.ts.net`). Substitute it wherever you see `your-tailnet.ts.net`.
  Likewise, `/home/you/` stands in for your own home directory, and `you@homelab`
  for your own username on the Pi.
- **Toy example values, swap in yours.** Some things need a concrete number to
  show, so the guide picks a running example and reuses it. The big ones are the
  Pi's home (LAN) IP, written as **`192.168.1.50`**, and the router, written as
  **`192.168.1.1`**. *Yours will almost certainly differ* (find the Pi's with
  `hostname -I`, the router's on the back of the router or in its app). Whenever
  you see one of these, read it as "e.g." and use your own. The same goes for any
  bare LAN IP in a command or screenshot.
- **Manual commands first, scripts beside them.** The guide shows the actual
  files and commands so you can see what is happening. The chapter folders also
  include numbered helper scripts (`01-...sh`, `02-...sh`, etc.) for the same
  file-creation and startup steps if you would rather automate the copy/paste.
  Those scripts are standalone: they write their files inline and do not depend
  on the `compose/` reference files. Read a script before running it; it is
  intentionally small enough to audit.

::: {.callout-note}
## Why Tailscale is the spine of this series
Every part avoids port forwarding, dynamic DNS, and exposing services to the
public internet. Instead, each service binds to the Pi and is reached over an
authenticated WireGuard mesh. The result: your homelab is private by default,
works on locked-down corporate Wi-Fi, and needs no router
configuration. You sign in once per device and the names resolve over the
tailnet.
:::

::: {.callout-important}
## Security & secrets: read before you publish or push anything
This guide is written to be **safe to commit to a public Git repo** and safe to
follow. Two habits make that true, and they're applied throughout:

- **Never put a real password, API token, or key in a file you commit.** Every
  config below reads its secrets from a local `.env` file that is listed in
  `.gitignore` and never leaves your machine.
- **Nothing is exposed to the public internet.** Services are reached only over
  your private tailnet or your home LAN, never by port-forwarding. Remote
  access to the Pi uses SSH over Tailscale with **key-based authentication**
  (set up in Chapter 1). Don't open router ports to any of this.

If you fork this guide for your own site, scrub any real tailnet names, IPs, and
usernames first, they're mildly identifying. This guide uses placeholders for
exactly that reason.
:::
